skill.name: agent-browser
skill.description: Automates browser sessions through the inference.sh belt CLI. It can open pages, inspect interactive elements, fill forms, click controls, capture screenshots, record video, route through proxies, upload files, and run page JavaScript.
skill.summary: Control browser sessions for web automation, testing, capture, and research workflows.
skill.author: qu-skills

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many command, URL, path, and credential findings; many are documentation false positives such as markdown code fences and placeholder URLs. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, but the intended feature set combines external CLI execution, arbitrary URL navigation, JavaScript execution, credential and cookie handling, proxies, screenshots, video, and file upload. The skill should receive human publisher review before marketplace release.

high_findings.title: Broad External Browser CLI Permission
high_findings.description: The skill grants Bash access for the belt CLI and teaches users to run browser sessions against arbitrary URLs. This is expected for the skill, but it is a real external command and network automation capability rather than a harmless documentation artifact.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and repeated command examples confirm real external CLI execution. The commands are constrained to belt or infsh, so the risk is high but not evidence of malicious intent.

high_findings.title: Unrestricted Page JavaScript Execution
high_findings.description: The execute function accepts caller-provided JavaScript and runs it in the active browser page context. On authenticated pages this can extract page content, links, session-visible data, and readable cookies.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly exposes a code field for JavaScript execution and includes cookie and page extraction examples. This is a confirmed dangerous capability, though it runs in browser context rather than host shell context.

high_findings.title: Credential and Session Data Exposure Workflows
high_findings.description: The authentication guide demonstrates entering passwords, OAuth credentials, 2FA codes, session reuse, cookie extraction, and printing cookies. These workflows can expose credentials or session tokens if used on untrusted sites or logged insecurely.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines directly show password entry, 2FA entry, and cookie extraction. The same file includes best-practice warnings, but the sensitive-data handling risk is still real.

high_findings.title: Proxy Rotation and Anonymity Guidance
high_findings.description: The proxy guide includes proxy rotation for web scraping, SOCKS proxy support, and privacy proxy examples. This is legitimate for testing, but it can also enable rate-limit evasion or policy bypass.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The guide explicitly labels a section as rate limit avoidance and provides rotating proxy logic. It also contains rate-limit-respect guidance, so the concern is abuse potential rather than confirmed malicious code.

medium_findings.title: Local File Upload Capability
medium_findings.description: The interact action can upload local file paths to a page file input. This can leak local files if an agent or user supplies sensitive paths or follows untrusted page instructions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The upload action and file_paths field are documented clearly. No automatic traversal was found, but the capability can move local files to remote sites.

medium_findings.title: Screenshots and Video May Capture Sensitive Data
medium_findings.description: The skill can take screenshots and record browser sessions, including page loads, typing, scrolling, and dynamic content. Sensitive pages can be captured if recording is enabled during login or admin workflows.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Video and screenshot capture are core documented features, and the documentation warns that video may capture sensitive information. The warning reduces intent risk but does not remove the data exposure risk.

medium_findings.title: Unescaped User Inputs in Shell JSON Templates
medium_findings.description: The shell templates interpolate user-provided URLs and options into JSON request bodies. Malformed input can break the request or alter JSON fields if users pass values containing quotes or control characters.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The templates use shell string interpolation inside JSON. This is common in examples, but it lacks JSON escaping for user-controlled values.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fence Command Detections Are Mostly Expected Examples
low_findings.description: Many static external-command findings point to markdown code fences showing how to use belt or infsh. They are documentation examples, not hidden scripts that run automatically when the skill is loaded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious backtick and command-substitution patterns occur inside fenced markdown examples. The examples still document real commands, but they are not auto-executed Ruby backticks.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Example Targets
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings are primarily example.com, app.example.com, httpbin.org, and similar documentation placeholders. They do not indicate covert network exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible examples used to explain browser automation options. No evidence found of a hidden malicious destination.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal and Weak Crypto Detections Are Documentation Noise
low_findings.description: Static findings for path traversal and weak cryptography are triggered by markdown links, headings, tables, or placeholder syntax. No evidence found of cryptographic operations or path traversal code in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are relative documentation links or prose, not file access logic or cryptographic implementation. This supports dismissal of those specific pattern matches.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Markers Not Found
low_findings.description: No evidence found for phrases that try to override system instructions, skip security analysis, claim pre-approval, or impersonate marketplace authority.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: A targeted search for common prompt injection phrases found no matching malicious instruction patterns. The cited front matter is ordinary skill metadata.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Permission for Browser CLI
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill declares Bash(belt *), enabling external browser automation through the belt CLI.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The permission is explicit in the skill front matter and matches the documented workflow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Arbitrary URL Navigation
dangerous_patterns.description: The open and goto flows allow navigation to user-supplied URLs, which creates network and policy risks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URL field is required for open and available for goto. This is a core capability with legitimate uses and abuse potential.

dangerous_patterns.title: Custom JavaScript Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The execute function runs caller-provided JavaScript and can extract DOM content from the active page.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documented code field and extraction templates directly confirm this pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cookie Extraction Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The authentication guide shows using JavaScript to retrieve document.cookie and echo the result.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: This is direct evidence of session data extraction from an authenticated browser context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Proxy-Based Traffic Routing
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill supports HTTP, HTTPS, SOCKS5, and authenticated proxies, including rotating proxy examples.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The proxy functionality is documented as a feature, with both legitimate testing and abuse-sensitive examples.

dangerous_patterns.title: File Upload From Local Paths
dangerous_patterns.description: The upload action accepts local file paths and sends selected files through a browser file input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The action and file_paths parameter are documented in both the overview and command reference.

content.value_statement: Manual browser tasks are slow to repeat and difficult to document. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured browser control with refs, screenshots, video, and reusable shell templates.
actual_capabilities: Opens arbitrary web pages through the agent-browser app and returns page state.
Maps interactive page elements to short refs for click, fill, select, hover, drag, and upload actions.
Captures viewport or full-page screenshots for verification and documentation.
Records browser sessions as video when recording is enabled at session start.
Runs custom JavaScript in the browser page context and returns extracted results.
Provides shell templates for form automation, authenticated sessions, and content capture.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh belt or infsh CLI and an authenticated account.
Element refs become stale after navigation or dynamic page changes.
The skill can handle sensitive pages, so users must control credentials, cookies, screenshots, and recordings carefully.
Browser automation may be restricted by site terms, bot defenses, CAPTCHAs, or access controls.

use_cases.title: Repeat Form Checks
use_cases.description: Open a form, identify refs, submit test data, and capture screenshots for validation.

use_cases.title: Capture Web Evidence
use_cases.description: Record a user journey or capture full-page screenshots for bug reports and stakeholder review.

use_cases.title: Extract Page Content
use_cases.description: Collect visible text, links, titles, and screenshots from selected pages for structured research.

prompt_templates.title: Open and Inspect a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-browser to open this URL, list the important interactive refs, and summarize the visible page state.

prompt_templates.title: Fill a Test Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Open this form, identify the required fields, fill them with safe test values, submit it, and capture the final page state.

prompt_templates.title: Capture a Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Record a browser session for this workflow, show the cursor, take a final screenshot, and close the session to retrieve the video.

prompt_templates.title: Authenticated Data Review
prompt_templates.prompt: After I provide approved credentials, use an authenticated browser session to review the dashboard, avoid recording secrets, and close the session when done.

output_examples.input: Open a product settings page and report the available controls.
output_examples.output: A concise page summary with named refs for buttons, inputs, links, and a screenshot reference for visual review.

output_examples.input: Submit a contact form with test data and verify the result.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step status report showing which fields were filled, whether submission succeeded, and what confirmation appeared.

output_examples.input: Capture evidence for a failed checkout flow.
output_examples.output: A written failure summary with the final page URL, visible error message, screenshot reference, and optional video reference.

best_practices: Use only approved target sites and follow their terms and rate limits.
Keep credentials in secure environment variables and avoid recording sensitive sessions.
Refresh snapshots after navigation, form submission, or dynamic page changes.
anti_patterns: Do not run the skill against unknown sites while using real account credentials.
Do not upload local files unless the target site and file path are explicitly approved.
Do not use proxy rotation to bypass access controls, rate limits, or site policies.

faq.question: What CLI does this skill use?
faq.answer: It uses the inference.sh belt or infsh command to run the remote agent-browser app.

faq.question: Can it interact with authenticated pages?
faq.answer: Yes, but credentials, cookies, screenshots, and videos must be treated as sensitive data.

faq.question: Does it support screenshots and video?
faq.answer: Yes. It can capture screenshots and record a full session when recording is enabled.

faq.question: Can it run JavaScript on a page?
faq.answer: Yes. The execute function runs custom JavaScript in the browser page context.

faq.question: What happens after a page changes?
faq.answer: Element refs may become stale, so take a fresh snapshot before the next action.

faq.question: Is this safe for public marketplace publishing?
faq.answer: It needs publisher review because it combines browser automation, external commands, network access, file upload, proxies, and credential workflows.
